system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

function.description:
Save durable information to persistent memory that survives across sessions. Memory is injected into future turns, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.

WHEN TO SAVE (do this proactively, don't wait to be asked):
- User corrects you or says 'remember this' / 'don't do that again'
- User shares a preference, habit, or personal detail (name, role, timezone, coding style)
- You discover something about the environment (OS, installed tools, project structure)
- You learn a convention, API quirk, or workflow specific to this user's setup
- You identify a stable fact that will be useful again in future sessions

PRIORITY: User preferences and corrections > environment facts > procedural knowledge. The most valuable memory prevents the user from having to repeat themselves.

Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts.
If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.

- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

Here is a breakdown of why this UI is visually failing:

### 1. The "Void" Aesthetic (Whitespace Abuse)
The most glaring issue is the **massive, soul-crushing amount of empty white space**.
*   **What I see:** A tiny cluster of buttons in the top left, a search bar floating in the top right, and a desert of nothingness in the middle.
*   **Why it's ugly:** Good design uses whitespace to create focus; this design uses whitespace because it’s unfinished. It feels hollow and unbalanced. The "No hay pedidos" (No orders) text is floating in the center like a lost thought.

### 2. The "Staircase" Alignment
The layout has no consistent grid or margin system.
*   **What I see:** The status buttons (Todos, pendiente, etc.) are slammed against the top-left corner with almost zero padding from the edge of the screen. Meanwhile, the search bar is floating in the top-right corner.

The buttons look like they were styled using the most basic, default CSS presets available.
*   **What I see:** Tiny, pill-shaped buttons with cramped text. The "Todos" button is a jarring, bright green that doesn't seem to match any other brand color, while the others are a depressing dark slate.
*   **Why it's ugly:** The lack of padding inside the buttons makes the text feel suffocated. There is no stylistic harmony between the "search" input (which is dark) and the "action" buttons.

### 4. Zero Branding or Context
If you didn't provide the URL, no one would know what this website is for.
*   **What I see:** No logo, no navigation bar, no header, and no "Admin" title.

# Standalone E-commerce Store

Build, audit, and deploy production Next.js 15 e-commerce stores.

## Architecture

Cloudflare (DNS, SSL) -> VPS -> Traefik -> service_web:3000

## Tech Stack

Next.js 15 (standalone output), React 19, Tailwind CSS 4, Docker + Swarm, Traefik, SQLite (optional)

## Build & Deploy

```
npm run build
docker build -t <name>:prod .
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml <name>
```

## Page Structure

Home, Tienda, Producto/[slug], Envio, FAQ, Contacto, Privacidad, Terminos, Nosotros, Promociones, Blog (posts + categories + [slug]), Sitemap, Error, Not-found, RSS

## Product Data Structure (Retail)

Each product should carry rich spec data, not just name+price:

```json
{
  "slug": "titanium",
  "name": "Titanium",
  "line": "Premium",
  "category": "Resorte",
  "price": "Gs. 1.800.000",
  "priceFrom": "2 plazas 140x190",
  "image": "/images/products/titanium.png",
  "description": "Premium con Euro Pillow Top.",
  "sizes": ["2 plazas 140x190: Gs. 1.800.000", "Queen 160x200: Gs. 2.200.000"],
  "firmness": "Firme",
  "warranty": "5 años",
  "weight": "Hasta 120 kg por lado",
  "colors": ["Gris", "Azul"],
  "badges": ["Pillow Top", "Antiácaros", "Más vendido"]
}
```

Product pages render a specs grid (firmness, warranty, weight, colors), a measure/price table, and badges.

## Retail-Specific Features

- **Bundles/Combos**: Section on homepage showing 2-3 product bundles with original price, bundle price, savings, and WhatsApp inquiry link
- **Promo banners**: Downloadable from client's current site, displayed as clickable 3-column grid in `#0F1624` section
- **Store locator**: GPS coordinates per store, clickable address → Google Maps, clickable phone → tel: link, type badges (Store/Mall/Outlet/Distribución), Argentina subsidiary section
- **Product page**: Specs grid (firmness, warranty, weight, colors), size/price table, badges row
- **Cross-sell**: "Complete your bundle" section — mattress + base + protector + pillows

## Critical Patterns

- WhatsApp-first commerce (all CTAs go to WhatsApp)
- Content-driven from content/es.json
- Age-gated content (localStorage with 7-day timestamp expiry)
- Discreet shipping guarantee (for adult stores)
- **Content layer over Supabase**: When Supabase tables don't exist, create `lib/content.ts` helpers that read from `content/es.json` directly (see `references/content-layer-pattern.md`). Don't fight missing DB — route around it.
**Single phone number source of truth**: `WA_NUMBER` in `lib/content.ts`, plus `getWhatsAppLink(text)` helper. Scan ALL source files for hardcoded numbers after any change (see `references/phone-number-batch-fix.md`).

## Content Structure

content/es.json sections: siteConfig, navigation, home, products (14+ with id/slug/name/price/level/sound/safety fields), categories, faq, blog, footer, shipping, payments, socialProof, brand

## Comprehensive Site Audit Checklist

Run on EVERY page. Be brutal.

### Audit Dimensions:

1. Visual/Design -- spacing, alignment, color, images, fonts, hierarchy, consistency
2. UX/Usability -- navigation, button placement, mobile, loading states, forms, click targets
3. Content -- copy quality, tone, grammar, completeness, placeholders, length
4. E-commerce -- conversion flow, trust signals, pricing, urgency, social proof, stock
5. Technical -- load speed, console errors, broken links, SEO, titles, structured data, a11y
6. Mobile -- viewport, tap targets, readability, nav overlap
7. Industry-specific -- privacy signals, discretion tone, age gate, comfort for first-timers
8. Cross-cutting -- image placeholders, inconsistent icons, duplication, text bugs, keyboard nav

### Severity Triage:

- CRITICAL -- Broken functionality, legal exposure, blank pages. Fix immediately.
- HIGH -- Trust-killing issues (no contact info, no product images, duplicate nav). Fix this batch.
- MEDIUM -- UX friction, redundant elements. Fix alongside HIGH.
- LOW -- Polish, accessibility extras. Fix when time permits.

### Batch Fix Strategy:

1. Fix all CRITICAL first (1-3 line fixes)
2. Fix all HIGH second (users notice these)
3. Fix MEDIUM alongside HIGH
4. Fix LOW last
5. Commit + deploy as single atomic batch

## Auth System: Supabase (Recommended)

See `references/supabase-auth-pattern.md` for:

### Client Files (4 required)` | `### Supabase client from content/es.json: Middleware breakage

The middleware runs at the edge layer, NOT in the Node.js runtime. When Supabase client files use `require("@/content/es.json")` or `import es from "@/content/es.json"` to read the Supabase URL/config, this works in browser/server components but **crashes middleware** with:

```text
Error: Your project's URL and Key are required to create a Supabase client!
```

**Fix: Guard middleware env-var-dependent logic:**
```ts
export async function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  if (!process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL || !process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY) {
    return NextResponse.next()
  }
  // ... create Supabase client with process.env values, NOT content/es.json
}
```

**Rule:** Middleware should use `process.env` directly, not imported JSON files. The content/es.json approach works for `'use client'` and server components, but NOT for middleware.

## Auth System: Supabase (Recommended)

See `references/supabase-auth-pattern.md` for:

### Client Files (4 required)
- `lib/supabase/server.ts` — SSR client using `@supabase/ssr`'s `createServerClient` with cookie store
- `lib/supabase/browser.ts` — Browser client using `createBrowserClient`
- `lib/supabase/client.ts` — Re-exports from browser.ts with singleton
- `lib/supabase/admin.ts` — Service role client for admin API routes (bypasses RLS)

### Middleware
```ts
// middleware.ts — refresh session on every request
import { createServerClient } from '@supabase/ssr'
import { NextResponse } from 'next/server'
import type { NextRequest } from 'next/server'

export async function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  // Guard: skip if env vars missing (prevents crash on deploy with bad config)
  if (!process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL) {
    return NextResponse.next()
  }
  // ... createServerClient with cookie getAll/setAll
  await supabase.auth.getUser()
  return supabaseResponse
}

export const config = {
  matcher: ['/((?!_next/static|_next/image|favicon\\.ico|.*\\.(?:svg|png|jpg|jpeg|gif|webp)$).*)'],
}
```

### AuthContext Pattern
- Wraps root or per-page in `<AuthProvider>`
- Listens to `supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange` for session tracking
- Fetches profile from `profiles` table on mount (not `auth.users()` directly)
- Provides `login`, `register`, `loginWithGoogle`, `loginWithFacebook`, `logout`
- Social login: `supabase.auth.signInWithOAuth({ provider: 'google', options: { redirectTo } })`
- Favors `getSession()` over `getUser()` for client-side (less overhead)

### OAuth Callback Route
```
app/auth/callback/route.ts — exchanges code for session, redirects to /mi-cuenta
```

### Header Auth Integration (Dynamic Login/MiCuenta Toggle)

The header must switch between login button and user menu based on Supabase session:

```
<Header> component:
  - On mount: supabase.auth.getSession() → fetch profile from profiles table
  - Subscribe to onAuthStateChange → update user state

  LOGGED OUT:
    - Green "Ingresar" button with person icon + text
    - Links to /login

  LOGGED IN:
    - Circle with user initials (e.g., "IV" from "Ivan V")
    - Hover dropdown: name, email, quick links (Mi Cuenta, Pedidos, Direcciones, Favoritos, Configuración)
    - Admin link (if role === 'admin'): "/admin" in green
    - Logout button in red
    - Mobile: user card with avatar + pill buttons for account/admin/logout
    - Mobile (logged out): full-width green "Iniciar Sesión / Registrarse" CTA
```

### Supabase Migration Process

When migrating from SQLite/localStorage to Supabase:

1. **Create tables via Dashboard** (can't do DDL via REST API without access token)
   - SQL file: `supabase/migrations/001_elviajero_schema.sql`
   - Run in Supabase Dashboard SQL Editor at `https://supabase.com/dashboard/project/{REF}/sql/new`

2. **Write Supabase client files** (server.ts, browser.ts, admin.ts, middleware.ts)

3. **Rewrite auth-context.tsx** — replace custom JWT/session logic with `supabase.auth.*`

4. **Rewrite API routes** — each route swaps SQLite queries for Supabase queries (addresses, orders, products, profile, password)

5. **Rewrite admin panel (biggest task)** — ALL admin pages reading from localStorage must be rewritten to query Supabase tables. This is always the bigger problem than auth migration (~5h vs ~2h).

6. **Seed script** — `scripts/seed.ts` inserts products from content/es.json + creates admin user via `supabase.auth.admin.createUser()`

7. **Update docker-compose.yml** — remove SQLite volume, add Supabase env vars, interpolation via shell exports (not .env file)

8. **OAuth providers** — configure Google + Facebook in Supabase Dashboard → Authentication → Providers. Site URL + redirect URL must match live domain.

### Personal Data Stripping for Artist Sites from Personal Repos

When building a public artist/band site from personal content (psychology repos, journals, letters):

**MUST strip before publishing:**
- Real names (family, partners, your own full name)
- Specific medical/health disclosures (HIV, medications, treatment details)
- Family role descriptions ("The Engineer", "child who wanted to be held" → "heart that wanted to be held")
- Place-specific references ("in my own home" → "in the peripheries")
- Specific biographical facts that could identify the person
- Age, location, employer, educational details

**Rewrite strategy:**
- Replace specific disclosures with universal emotional truths
- "I am not a risk / I am not a disease" → "I am not my past / I am not my shame"
- "My family didn't know what to do with me" → "I learned young that feelings were a dangerous thing"
- Keep the emotional core intact while removing identifying specifics

**Checklist:** run `grep -rni 'family|parents|mother|father|hiv|positive|diagnos|weiss|real name'` on all site files before deploy.

### Songs/Lyrics Content Pattern

For artist sites with original music, use a `src/data/songs.ts` data module instead of es.json:

Then render an **accordion/expandable lyrics section** — clicking a song title expands to show full lyrics beneath it. Use `useState` to track which song is open.

```tsx
// Key pattern for lyrics accordion:
const [openSong, setOpenSong] = useState<string | null>(null)
const toggleSong = (id: string) => setOpenSong(openSong === id ? null : id)

// Each song row is a <button onClick={toggleSong(song.id)}> that toggles a collapsible div
// The div uses animate-in: fade-in slide-in-from-top-2
// Inside the expanded panel: h-px divider + lyrics as whitespace-pre-line text
```

### Interactive Artist Site Sections (Beyond Content-Driven Pages)

For band/artist sites that need revenue and engagement features, build these as section components + data files:

#### Audio Player / Tracklist (`music-section.tsx`)

A tracklist with functional in-browser audio player:

**Two-phase setup:**
1. Generate demo MP3s using Python tone synthesis + ffmpeg (see `references/audio-player-pattern.md` for full code)
2. Wire the `<audio>` player component (same reference)

**Data source:** `src/data/songs.ts` — array of { id, title, subtitle, lyrics, genre, duration, mood }

**Tracklist rows:** Each track is a clickable row: play button (circle with triangle icon), track number, title, subtitle, genre, duration. Clicking expands the player panel below.

**Click interaction:** clicking a track row sets `playingId` state, which conditionally renders an expanded audio player with:
- Song info header (emoji icon, title, genre/duration/mood metadata)
- **Functional HTML5 audio player** (see `references/audio-player-pattern.md` for component code):
  - Waveform progress bar (80 bars, clickable for seeking)
  - Play/pause button
  - Time counter (m:ss / m:ss)
  - Volume slider
  - Hidden `<audio>` element with `preload="metadata"`
- "Read lyrics" link scrolling to `#lyrics` for the active song

**Streaming links:** `src/data/socials.ts` with { name, url, color } — badge-style links with colored dot, rendered above the tracklist.

**Animations:** `animate-in fade-in slide-in-from-top-2` on player panel, pulse bars on the mini-player when "playing".

**Audio file placement:** MP3s go in `public/audio/{song-id}.mp3`. The Dockerfile's `COPY --from=builder /app/public ./public` includes them automatically in standalone builds.

**Alternative to real recordings:** When no actual recordings exist, use the Python tone synthesis pattern to generate mood-matched demo tracks (bass drone + warm pad + arpeggio + soft percussion). See the reference file for the full 4-layer generation algorithm with mood-specific shaping.

#### Events / Live Shows (`events-section.tsx`)

Two-tier structure:
- Data source: `src/data/events.ts` — `Event[]` with id, title, date (ISO), venue, city, country, status (upcoming|past|cancelled), ticketUrl, ticketPrice, description, lineup, soldOut
- **Upcoming shows**: Cards with date block (day/month/year), vertical divider, venue/city/lineup/description, ticket CTA button (price or "TBA" or "Sold Out"). Sold shows get `opacity-60` treatment
- **Past shows**: Collapsible accordion (toggle button: "Show past shows (N)"). Past entries rendered simpler — date + title + venue
- Status badges per card: green "Upcoming", red "Sold Out", muted "Past"
- Empty state: "No shows scheduled yet" centered card

#### Merch Shop (`merch-section.tsx`)

WhatsApp-first catalog with no payment gateway:
- Data source: `src/data/merch.ts` — { id, name, price: { pyg, usd }, sizes[], color, description, waTemplate, image, category }
- Products grid (sm:2, lg:3 columns) with image placeholder (emoji per category + "Photo coming soon"), name, price, description (2-line clamp), size selector buttons, WhatsApp order button
- Category filter: "All", "Apparel", "Accessories", "Digital", "Posters" buttons — active state uses primary brand color
- Size selector: product.sizes mapped to small toggle buttons, first size selected by default
- WhatsApp order: builds template message from waTemplate, replaces [SIZE], opens wa.me link
- Categories in data file: array of { id, label } for expandability

#### Social Links / Connect (`contact-section.tsx` variant)

Dual-panel layout (md:grid-cols-2):
- Left: "Follow" panel — socials data array rendered as list items with SVG icon (per-platform inline SVG), name, handle, external link arrow
- Right: "Get in Touch" panel — WhatsApp card (green bg-tint with WA icon) + Email card (with mail icon)

### SQLite Schema (must include sessions table)

The schema in `lib/db.ts` needs a `sessions` table for token-based auth:```
## Lead-to-Site Batch Deploy Script

See `scripts/deploy-lead-site.sh` — reusable script for deploying a single lead site to VPS. Run it individually per site (not in a loop) to avoid SSH timeout on long builds:

```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS sessions (
  token TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id TEXT NOT NULL,
  expires_at TEXT NOT NULL,
  FOREIGN KEY (user_id) REFERENCES users(id)
);
```

### Auth context patterns

1. **Always wrap children in AuthContext.Provider** — never conditionally render a fallback during loading/SSR. Children will try to call `useAuth()` and crash. Set all initial state to empty/null values.

2. **Guard localStorage** — check `typeof window !== "undefined"` before any `localStorage.getItem()` call. SSR runs this code.

3. **API helper with bearer token** — define a helper function that reads token from localStorage and attaches `Authorization: Bearer` header. This avoids repeating the pattern in every method.

4. **Auth routes pattern** — single POST /api/auth route with `action` field dispatching to login/register/me/logout branches. Rate limit with in-memory map keyed on `email:ip`.

5. **Addresses/Orders as separate routes** — PUT/DELETE on /api/addresses for CRUD, GET/POST on /api/orders. Authenticate by reading session token from `Authorization` header.

6. **Dynamic export** — all pages that use `useAuth` need `export const dynamic = "force-dynamic"` to prevent Next.js 15 from trying to prerender them during build (will crash with "useAuth must be used within AuthProvider").

### Next.js Standalone + Docker: outputFileTracingRoot Trap

When workspaces have multiple `package-lock.json`, Next.js prints a warning about workspace root detection. The suggested fix `outputFileTracingRoot: "/root"` works for local builds but **breaks Docker builds** — the standalone directory becomes nested (`standalone/repo-name/server.js` instead of `standalone/server.js`), and the COPY in Dockerfile fails.

**Rule:** Never add `outputFileTracingRoot` in projects deployed via Docker. The warning is harmless — the standalone build still works correctly inside the Docker container. Only use it for non-Docker deployments (bare-metal, Cloudflare Pages).

### Docker COPY Pattern

```dockerfile
# STANDALONE directory structure depends on build environment:
#   Local with outputFileTracingRoot: /root/standalone/repo-name/server.js
#   Docker with plain output: standalone: /app/.next/standalone/server.js (FLAT)

# WRONG — tied to one environment
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone/repo-name ./

# RIGHT — copies whatever structure exists
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
```

When switching from a custom base image (`paragu-ai/next-base:node20`) to standard `node:20-alpine`, the standalone directory layout changes. Always verify with:
```bash
docker run --rm <builder-stage> find /app/.next/standalone -maxdepth 2 -name "*.js"
```

## Standalone Next.js Docker Build: Directory Structure Mismatch

The `.next/standalone/` directory STRUCTURE changes based on the build environment:

| Environment | Structure |
|-------------|-----------|
| Local `npm run build` | `standalone/{package-name}/server.js` |
| Docker `node:20-alpine` | `standalone/server.js` (FLAT) |
| Docker custom base (paragu-ai/next-base) | Varies by image |

**Never hardcode the subdirectory path in Dockerfile COPY.** Instead, copy the entire standalone directory and the server.js will be at the WORKDIR root:

```dockerfile
# WRONG — assumes nested path
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone/elviajero-comercio ./

# RIGHT — copies whatever structure exists
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
```

**Build verification:** Always test with a full Docker build after any Dockerfile change. Local-only testing (`npm run build`) does NOT validate the Docker COPY paths.

## Dockerfile: Environment Variable for Next.js Standalone

The Dockerfile runner stage MUST include:
```dockerfile
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
ENV PORT=3000
```
Without `HOSTNAME=0.0.0.0`, Next.js binds to the container hostname and curls return 000 timeout.

## Dockerfile: Multi-stage with node:20-alpine Healthcheck

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS deps
WORKDIR /app
RUN apk add --no-cache libc6-compat
COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm ci --legacy-peer-deps

FROM node:20-alpine AS builder
WORKDIR /app
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
RUN npm run build

FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=production
ENV NEXT_TELEMETRY_DISABLED=1
ENV PORT=3000
ENV HOSTNAME="0.0.0.0"
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
USER nextjs
EXPOSE 3000
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/standalone ./
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/.next/static ./.next/static
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/public ./public
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/content ./content
COPY --from=builder --chown=nextjs:nodejs /app/data ./data
CMD node server.js
```

The base image `paragu-ai/next-base:node20` has `ENV NODE_ENV=production`. This causes `npm install` in the deps stage to skip devDependencies including `typescript`, which Next.js 15 needs to parse `next.config.ts`.

Fix: Set `ENV NODE_ENV=development` in the deps stage:

```dockerfile
# MANDATORY: next.config.ts must have output: "standalone"
# Without this, .next/standalone/server.js won't exist and container crashes on start
FROM paragu-ai/next-base:node20 AS deps
WORKDIR /app
ENV NODE_ENV=development
COPY package.json package-lock.json* ./
RUN npm install --legacy-peer-deps
```

Keep the builder stage at `NODE_ENV=production` (devDeps already installed by deps stage).

### Cloudflare SSL Mode for New Subdomains

New proxied (orange cloud) subdomains default to **Full (Strict)** mode in Cloudflare. This rejects self-signed origin certs with error 526.

Fix: In Cloudflare Dashboard → SSL/TLS → Overview, change from "Full (Strict)" to **"Full"** (accepts any origin cert). Or generate a Cloudflare Origin CA cert.

ACME HTTP-01 challenge does NOT work through Cloudflare proxy — Let's Encrypt hits CF edge IPs, not the VPS. Use DNS challenge or temporary DNS-only mode for ACME.

### Batch Site Generation from Leads

See `references/python-site-generator-pattern.md` for the Python script pattern that generates 5+ structurally-identical sites from data dicts. Use when deploying multiple leads from `paragu-ai-leads` repo.

### Next.js 16 TypeScript Quirks

When adding new Supabase tables that aren't in `src/types/database.ts`, every query against them gets type `never`. Quick fix: `// @ts-nocheck` **must be the very first line** of the file (before `'use client'`, imports, everything).

Next.js 16 `middleware.ts` shows a deprecation warning — use `proxy` in future. Still functional now.

### Edge Middleware + Env Vars in Docker Swarm

**Critical pitfall:** The middleware runs at the edge layer, NOT in the Node.js runtime. If it calls `createServerClient(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!, ...)`, it needs those env vars available at *runtime* — build-time `--build-arg` is not enough.

**Fix:** Guard env-var-dependent logic so the middleware doesn't crash when vars are missing:
```ts
export async function middleware(request: NextRequest) {
  if (!process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL || !process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY) {
    return NextResponse.next()
  }
  // ... create Supabase client
}
```

Without this guard, the middleware crashes on startup if env vars aren't available — and since the middleware runs on EVERY page load, the entire site goes down.

### Supabase Management API: Running Raw SQL

With a **Supabase personal access token** (`sbp_...`), you can execute raw SQL directly:

```bash
export SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN="sbp_..."
curl -s -X POST "[REDACTED_SUPABASE_URL]m/v1/projects/{PROJECT_REF}/database/query" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ..."}'
```

This is the only way to run DDL from CLI without a direct PSQL connection. Get the token from https://supabase.com/dashboard/account/tokens.

### Builder Schema Conflict Pattern

When the Supabase project is shared with `paragu-ai-builder` (or any multi-tenant system), existing tables like `products`, `orders`, `reviews` have a **different schema** with columns like `business_id`, `price_cents`, `inventory_qty` — not the simple `name`, `price`, `stock` pattern your code expects.

**Solutions (in preference order):**

1. **Separate table names** — prefix with `ej_` (e.g., `ej_products`, `ej_orders`). Avoids collision entirely. Create them via the Management API SQL endpoint above.

2. **Adapt to builder schema** — map your data to the builder's columns (price → price_cents, stock → inventory_qty, etc.). Requires a `business_id` from the businesses table.

3. **Skip existing tables** — use builder's products table for read-only and keep your own order/review logic separate.

**When script/seed.ts runs:**
```bash
# Products table might have 2500+ rows from builder tenants
# Check before inserting
const { count } = await supabase.from('products').select('*', { count: 'exact', head: true })
if (count === 0) { /* seed */ }
```

### Supabase Service Role + Migration

When deploying to an existing Supabase project (not the one in your `.env`), verify which project the site actually points to. The `paragu-ai-builder/.env` at `qyvokpribmbrosafntqa.supabase.co` might be a different project than the one powering the deployed site. Always check:
```bash
# Check which Supabase project is being used
docker exec container-name env | grep SUPABASE_URL
# Or check compiled output for hardcoded URL
grep -r 'supabase.co' .next/
```

The site's 001_initial_schema.sql might have been run manually or through a different pipeline. RPC functions like `validate_coupon` and `is_ci_blacklisted` won't exist until migration is run. Check with:
```bash
curl -s -X POST "${SUPABASE_URL}/rest/v1/rpc/is_ci_blacklisted" \
  -H "apikey: ${ANON_KEY}" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"p_ci_number":"123"}'
```
If `PGRST202` — the function doesn't exist, migration hasn't run.

### Swarm Docker Compose

```yaml
# DON'T — stack deploy rejects both
build: .
mem_limit: 512m
container_name: my-app
# DON'T — bridge network, Swarm needs overlay
networks:
  my-net:
    name: my-net  # creates bridge by default

# DO — use pre-built image, deploy.resources for limits
image: my-app:prod
deploy:
  resources:
    limits:
      memory: 512m
```

Swarm stacks don't support `build:`, `container_name:`, or `mem_limit:` — those get ignored/rejected. Always pre-build the image with `docker build -t my-app:prod .` then reference `image: my-app:prod` in the compose.

### Swarm network: overlay, not bridge
`docker network create --driver overlay --attachable my-net` for Swarm services. Bridge networks are local scope and Swarm rejects them with "not in the right scope: local instead of swarm".

If a bridge network already exists with the same name (`docker network ls` shows it), you can't create an overlay with the same name. Remove the old bridge network first, or use a different name for the overlay network. Existing Swarm services already have overlay networks; check with `docker network ls --filter driver=overlay`.

### Shell env vars + Swarm deploy

Docker does NOT read `.env` files for `docker stack deploy` (that's a `docker compose` feature). Always:

```bash
export VAR1="value1"
export VAR2="value2"
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml my-stack
```

For Supabase env vars, the deploy script must export them before calling `docker stack deploy`:
```bash
export SUPABASE_ANON_KEY="...your anon key..."
export SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY="...your service role key..."
docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml elviajero-comercio
```

If env vars aren't exported, the Docker container starts but immediately crashes with:
```
Error: Your project's URL and Key are required to create a Supabase client!
```

The middleware.ts instantiation fails because `createServerClient(process.env.NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL!)` receives empty strings at runtime.

### Detecting Missing Env Vars in Running Containers

```bash
# Check which Supabase URL a running container actually uses
docker exec $(docker ps --filter name=<stack> -q | head -1) env | grep SUPABASE

# Docker inspect REDACTS values to empty — don't trust it
docker inspect container-name --format '{{json .Config.Env}}'  # Shows empty strings for sensitive vars
```
Docker does NOT read `.env` files for `docker stack deploy` (that's a `docker compose` feature). Always:

```bash
source /path/to/.env && docker stack deploy -c compose.yml my-stack
```

Or pass via `docker service update --env-add VAR=value` after deploy. But note: Docker inspect **redacts** env var values to empty strings in CLI output for security — don't be misled thinking they're empty.

### Next.js standalone + Swarm healthcheck
Next.js 16 standalone image works with healthcheck:
```yaml
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD", "wget", "--spider", "-q", "http://127.0.0.1:3000"]
  interval: 30s
  timeout: 10s
  retries: 3
```
But env vars MUST be passed to the service (not just Dockerfile build args) because middleware.ts runs at edge and needs `NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL`/`KEY` at runtime. Without them, the container starts but crashes immediately with "Your project's URL and Key are required".

## Repo Merge Pattern (Two Divergent Codebases)

When merging two versions of the same site (e.g., old static Next 15 + new Supabase-backed Next 16):

### Assessment Phase
1. Catalog BOTH repos fully — file trees, configs, pages, components, dependencies
2. Identify overlapping components (header, footer, product-card, search) — these need replacing, not duplicating
3. Identify unique-to-each features — blog, admin panel, cart/checkout, 45 UX components
4. Count everything — total pages, total components, total products, unique design elements

### Merge Strategy: NEW as Base, LIVE Assets as Overlay
- The repo with the stronger backend (auth, DB, API routes, admin) becomes the base
- The repo with more UX polish (animations, blog, privacy features, SEO) contributes components/assets
- NEVER try to graft a backend onto a static site — too many breaking changes

### File Placement Convention
- LIVE UX components go in `src/components/vendor/` — they're imports, not core architecture
- Overlapping files (header, footer) get REPLACED, not merged
- Overlapping files may import each other's components — fix import paths after copy

### Import Path Fixing (Batch)
- Use `tsconfig.json` paths alias: `"@/*": ["./src/*"]`
- For content files at repo root: `"@/content/*": ["./content/*"]`
- After copying all files, grep for `@/components/` and `@/lib/` — fix every unresolved import
- LIVE components often import `./safe-image` or `./wishlist-button` as relative — need changing to `@/components/vendor/...`

### Domain Migration
When changing canonical domain (e.g., `sunstein.cloud` → `paragu-ai.com`):
1. Update `layout.tsx` — `metadataBase`, `og:url`, json-ld `"url"`
2. Update `constants.ts` — `SITE_URL`
3. Update `sitemap.ts`, `robots.ts`
4. Search ALL files for old domain — `grep -r` across entire `src/`
5. Use `sed -i` not `read_file+write_file` to avoid corruption (see pitfall below)

### Traefik: Container Labels vs Service Labels
When updating a Docker Swarm service's Traefik labels:
- `docker service update --label-add traefik.http.routers.X.rule=...` — adds to SERVICE labels (not seen by Traefik)
- `docker service update --container-label-add traefik.http.routers.X.rule=...` — adds to CONTAINER labels (read by Traefik)
- ALWAYS use `--container-label-add` for Traefik labels in Swarm mode
- Check with: `docker inspect container-name --format '{{json .Config.Labels}}'`
- If Traefik shows "Router defined multiple times", two containers claim the same router name — remove the old container

### Site Down DNS + Traefik Diagnosis

When a deployed site returns 404/000/refused:
1. `dig +short <domain>` — empty means no CF DNS record, CF IPs mean DNS exists
2. `docker service inspect <name> --format '{{range $k,$v := .Spec.Labels}}{{$k}}={{$v}}{{"\n"}}{{end}}' | grep traefik.http.routers` — check router rule
3. `docker exec $(docker ps --filter name=<name> -q head -1) wget -qO- http://127.0.0.1:3000/` — test internal health
4. If internal works but external doesn't, fix Traefik label domain mismatch or add CF DNS A record
5. `docker stack deploy -c docker-compose.yml <stack>` redeploys with updated labels

### Dead Supabase Project → Self-Hosted Postgres

When the Supabase project URL in `.env` returns NXDOMAIN, the project has been deleted/expired. See `references/supabase-to-selfhosted-pg-migration.md` for the full migration recipe covering:
- Combining + stripping Supabase-specific SQL (auth.users FKs, RLS, storage buckets, auth triggers)
- Applying via psql on Docker overlay network
- Environment updates and limitations

Key commands discovered in Fun4Me session:
```bash
# Strip auth refs
sed -i 's/REFERENCES auth\.users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE//g' /tmp/combined.sql
sed -i '/ENABLE ROW LEVEL SECURITY/d' /tmp/combined.sql
# Apply with error tolerance for storage/auth
psql -h <pg-ip> -U postgres -d <dbname> -v ON_ERROR_STOP=0 -f /schema.sql
```

### Docker Healthcheck: `wget` NOT in `node:20-alpine`

The `node:20-alpine` base image does NOT include `wget` by default. The healthcheck `test: ["CMD", "wget", "--spider", "http://127.0.0.1:3000/"]` silently fails — Swarm marks the task as unhealthy and rounds-robin restarts it indefinitely (stuck in "Starting" state forever).

**Fix:** Install wget in the runner stage before switching to non-root user:

```dockerfile
FROM node:20-alpine AS runner
# ... other setup ...
RUN apk add --no-cache wget
RUN addgroup --system --gid 1001 nodejs && \
    adduser --system --uid 1001 nextjs
USER nextjs
```

Without this, the service deploys but never passes healthcheck — `docker service ps` shows "Starting" indefinitely and the site returns 404 through Traefik.

### Supabase Management API: Running Raw SQL

With a **Supabase personal access token** (`sbp_...`), you can execute raw SQL directly via the Management API without a direct PSQL connection:

```bash
export SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN="sbp_..."
curl -s -X POST "[REDACTED_SUPABASE_URL]m/v1/projects/{PROJECT_REF}/database/query" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $SUPABASE_ACCESS_TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query":"CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS ..."}'
```

JSON-escaped SQL strings can be tricky. For multi-statement DDL, write a Node.js script that splits statements on `;` and executes them individually via fetch() to this endpoint. The endpoint handles each statement separately.

Get the token from https://supabase.com/dashboard/account/tokens.

### Shared Supabase Project: Builder Schema Conflict

When the Supabase project is shared with `paragu-ai-builder` (multi-tenant Next.js website generator), existing tables like `products`, `orders`, `reviews` have a **completely different schema**:

| El Viajero expects | Builder has |
|---|---|
| `products: name, price, stock, category` | `products: business_id, slug, price_cents, inventory_qty, images[], search_haystack` |
| `orders: id, user_id, items (json), total` | `orders: id, business_id, user_id, total_cents, shipping_address (json), tracking info` |
| `reviews: id, product_name, rating, text` | `reviews: id, business_id, product_id, author_name, content, is_approved` |

The builder tables also have foreign key constraints (e.g., `products.business_id → businesses.id`) that block direct inserts.

**Solution: Separate table prefix.** Create your own tables prefixed to avoid collision:
```sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS public.ej_products (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY DEFAULT gen_random_uuid(),
  name TEXT NOT NULL,
  category TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '',
  price TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT '0',
  -- simple schema, no builder constraints
);
```

Create via Management API SQL endpoint above. Then update all code references (API routes, admin pages, auth context) to use `ej_products` instead of `products`.

**Seed consideration:** The builder's `products` table may have 2500+ rows from other tenants. Always check count before seeding:
```ts
const { count } = await supabase.from('ej_products').select('*', { count: 'exact', head: true })
if (count === 0) { /* seed */ }
```

### Unified Checkout Route Pattern

Instead of creating per-gateway routes (`/api/checkout/pagopar`, `/api/checkout/bancard`, etc.), use a **single unified route** that dispatches by payment method:

```ts
// /app/api/checkout/route.ts — single POST handler
const gateway = method || process.env.PYG_PAYMENT_GATEWAY || 'pagopar'

if (gateway === 'pagopar')   return handlePagopar(order, items, total, customer)
if (gateway === 'bancard')   return handleBancard(order, total)
if (gateway === 'stripe')    return handleStripe(items, total, customer)
if (gateway === 'whatsapp')  return { ok: true, method: 'whatsapp', redirectUrl: '...' }
```

Each handler includes a **sandbox fallback**: if the API keys aren't configured, they return a WhatsApp fallback response instead of an error:

```ts
async function handlePagopar(...) {
  if (!PAGOPAR_PUBLIC_KEY || !PAGOPAR_PRIVATE_KEY) {
    return { ok: true, sandbox: true, method: 'whatsapp',
      message: 'Te contactamos por WhatsApp para coordinar el pago',
      redirectUrl: `/pedido/confirmado?id=${orderId}` }
  }
  // ... real Pagopar API call
}
```

This way the site works without any payment keys configured — it falls back to WhatsApp ordering. Set `PYG_PAYMENT_GATEWAY=pagopar` in env to change the default.

### Dynamic Products with JSON Fallback Pattern

When migrating from static JSON products to DB-backed products, make components fetch from DB first with a fallback to the build-time JSON:

```tsx
const [dbProducts, setDbProducts] = useState<any[]>([])
const supabase = createClient()

useEffect(() => {
  supabase.from("ej_products").select("*").order("name").then(({ data }) => {
    if (data && data.length > 0) {
      setDbProducts(data.map(p => ({
        name: p.name, category: p.category, price: p.price,
        priceBefore: p.price_before, // snap to camelCase for component compatibility
        imageUrl: p.image_url,
        isNew: p.is_new,
      })))
    }
  })
}, [supabase])

// DB takes priority, JSON is fallback
const allProducts = dbProducts.length > 0 ? dbProducts : staticProducts
```

This works because: the DB has the same data shape as the JSON (just different naming convention), the component only renders after client hydration, and the fallback ensures the page always shows something even if Supabase is down.

Key mapping: `ej_products` uses `snake_case` (PostgreSQL default), but the components expect `camelCase`. Always map in the fetch callback.

### Password Reset via Supabase (Replace LocalStorage/SQLite)

The old pattern used localStorage tokens with manual expiry. Supabase handles this:
- **Forgot password page:** `supabase.auth.resetPasswordForEmail(email, { redirectTo })` — sends actual email
- **Reset password page (token route):** On mount, check `supabase.auth.getSession()` — if session exists, user came from the reset link. Then `supabase.auth.updateUser({ password })`.
- **No token extraction needed** — Supabase's `@supabase/ssr` middleware exchanges the hash token for a session automatically on the callback URL.

```tsx
// /recuperar/page.tsx — request reset
const { error } = await supabase.auth.resetPasswordForEmail(email, {
  redirectTo: `${window.location.origin}/recuperar/confirmar`,
})

// /recuperar/[token]/page.tsx — no token extraction!
// Supabase middleware handles the exchange. Just check session:
const { data: { session } } = await supabase.auth.getSession()
if (session) { /* user can now update password */ }
```

Remove all old localStorage token logic (`viajero_users`, `resetToken`, `resetExpires`).

### P0 Fix: Admin Pages Using Anon Key Instead of Service Role

When admin pages use `createClient()` (browser client with anon key) to query admin tables like `ej_products`, `ej_orders`, they get 401 because the anon key has no RLS policies for these tables.

**Fix wiring pattern:**

1. Create API routes at `/app/api/admin/[resource]/route.ts` that use `getAdminClient()` (service_role key) to bypass RLS
2. Update admin pages to `fetch("/api/admin/products")` instead of `supabase.from("ej_products").select("*")`
3. The route handlers serve as a proxy: client → fetch → API route (service_role) → Supabase

**Example route handler (products):**
```ts
import { NextRequest, NextResponse } from "next/server"
import { getAdminClient } from "@/lib/supabase/admin"

const TABLE = "ej_products"

export async function GET() {
  const supabase = getAdminClient()
  const { data, error } = await supabase.from(TABLE).select("*").order("name")
  if (error) return NextResponse.json({ error: error.message }, { status: 500 })
  return NextResponse.json(data ?? [])
}

export async function POST(req: NextRequest) {
  const supabase = getAdminClient()
  const body = await req.json()
  const { data, error } = await supabase.from(TABLE).insert([body]).select()
  if (error) return NextResponse.json({ error: error.message }, { status: 500 })
  return NextResponse.json(data?.[0] ?? null)
}

export async function PATCH(req: NextRequest) {
  const supabase = getAdminClient()
  const body = await req.json()
  const { id, ...updates } = body
  if (!id) return NextResponse.json({ error: "id required" }, { status: 400 })
  const { data, error } = await supabase.from(TABLE).update(updates).eq("id", id).select()
  if (error) return NextResponse.json({ error: error.message }, { status: 500 })
  return NextResponse.json(data?.[0] ?? null)
}

export async function DELETE(req: NextRequest) {
  const supabase = getAdminClient()
  const id = new URL(req.url).searchParams.get("id")
  if (!id) return NextResponse.json({ error: "id required" }, { status: 400 })
  const { error } = await supabase.from(TABLE).delete().eq("id", id)
  if (error) return NextResponse.json({ error: error.message }, { status: 500 })
  return NextResponse.json({ success: true })
}
```

**Admin page before:**
```tsx
import { createClient } from "@/lib/supabase/client"
const supabase = createClient()
supabase.from("ej_products").select("*").then(({ data }) => { ... })
```

**After:**
```tsx
fetch("/api/admin/products").then(r => r.json()).then(data => { ... })
```

**PATCH endpoints** need `id` in the body, not URL.
**DELETE endpoints** use `?id=` in URL.

Create routes for: products, orders, promos, reviews, b2b, stats (dashboard counts).
The `lib/supabase/admin.ts` reads `SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY` from `process.env`.

**Supabase key rotation pitfall:** The anon key and service_role key in `.env.local` and `content/es.json` both become invalid when the Supabase project regenerates them. Symptoms: all Supabase queries return "Invalid API key". Fix: get new keys from Supabase Dashboard → Project Settings → API, update both `.env.local` and `content/es.json`. See `references/supabase-key-rotation-pitfall.md`.

**New key format (2026):** Supabase now ships keys as `sb_publishable_...` (anon) and `sb_secret_...` (service_role) instead of the old `eyJhbGci...` JWT format. Old keys still work for existing projects but new projects get the new format.

## User Lifecycle Automation (E-Commerce)

When building post-purchase flows for an e-commerce site, the pattern is:

### 1. Order Status WhatsApp to Customer
- Admin changes order status in panel → PATCH API route updates `ej_orders` status column
- If `updates.status` is set AND `data[0].customer_phone` exists, call `notifyStatusChange()`
- The server-side PATCH handler imports the WhatsApp lib dynamically:
  ```ts
  const { notifyStatusChange } = await import("@/lib/whatsapp")
  await notifyStatusChange(id, data[0].customer_phone, updates.status)
  ```

### 2. New Order WhatsApp to Admin
- Checkout places order via POST to `/api/admin/orders` (not direct `supabase.from().insert()`)
- The POST handler saves the order AND calls `notifyNewOrder()` with order details + admin panel link
- Customer data (name, phone, email) must be saved in the order for later notifications

### 3. Checkout Saves Customer Contact Info
```ts
// Instead of direct supabase insert, call the API route
const res = await fetch("/api/admin/orders", {
  method: "POST",
  headers: { "Content-Type": "application/json" },
  body: JSON.stringify({
    id: orderId, user_id: user?.id, items, total, status: "pendiente",
    payment_method, note,
    customer_name: customer.name,
    customer_phone: customer.phone || addressForm.phone || user?.phone || "",
    customer_email: customer.email,
  }),
})
```

### 4. Review Request + Abandoned Cart Cron
Create a single `/api/lifecycle` endpoint that:
- Checks `ej_orders` for delivered orders > 3 days → sends WhatsApp review request
- Checks `abandoned_carts` for unrecovered carts > 1 hour (reminder 1) or > 24 hours (reminder 2 with discount)
- Returns counts: `{ reviewRequests: N, abandonedReminders: M }`
- Schedule as a cron job every hour
- Use `sendWhatsApp()` from the Evolution API lib for messages

### 5. Admin Order Update Triggers Customer Notification
The PATCH handler for orders must detect status changes and send WhatsApp:
```ts
export async function PATCH(req: NextRequest) {
  const { id, ...updates } = await req.json()
  const { data } = await supabase.from("ej_orders").update(updates).eq("id", id).select()
  if (updates.status && data?.[0]?.customer_phone) {
    const { notifyStatusChange } = await import("@/lib/whatsapp")
    await notifyStatusChange(id, data[0].customer_phone, updates.status)
  }
  return NextResponse.json(data?.[0] ?? null)
}
```

## Admin Panel Feature Development (P0-P3 Prioritization)

When building admin features for an e-commerce site, use this priority framework derived from real owner feedback. Features are ranked by how much time/annoyance they save the business owner daily.

### P0 — Critical (owner uses daily, saves 1-2 hours/day)

These directly reduce owner workload and prevent revenue loss:

| Feature | Time | Why it matters |
|---------|------|---------------|
| **New Order WhatsApp Notification** | 2h | Order comes in → WhatsApp hits owner's phone. Paraguay runs on WhatsApp. Owner checks WA 50x/day, email 2x/day. Without this, orders sit for hours. |
| **Order Status WhatsApp to Customer** | 1h | Customer messages "ya salio mi pedido?" constantly. This eliminates those messages. Owner saves 10+ replies/day. |
| **Low Stock WhatsApp Alert** | 2h | Physical store + online stockouts lose sales. Alert when stock < threshold. |
| **Packing Slip / Invoice Print** | 3h | Owner writes packing slips by hand. One click saves 2-3 min/order. 5-10 orders/day = 15-30 min saved daily. |

### CRITICAL: read_file + write_write_file Corruption Trap

See `references/known-pitfalls.md` section on read_file corruption — the returned content has line-number prefixes that get embedded into the file on write_file(). Always prefer `patch()` or `sed -i` for edits.

## Common Pitfalls:

### CRITICAL: read_file + write_file Corrupts Files with Line-Number Prefix
`read_file()` returns content with `    N|` line-number prefixes embedded. When you write this content back with `write_file()`, the prefixes become part of the file. Subsequent reads compound the corruption: `   1|   2|   3|import...`.

**Symptoms:** Build errors with lines showing `    1|     1|import...`

**Prevention:**
- When editing content, use `patch()` (doesn't re-read full content) or `sed -i` (pure string replacement, no corruption risk)
- If you must use read/write, verify the file is clean afterward
- Fix corrupted files: `sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*[0-9]\+| //' file.tsx`
- After fixing, commit immediately — the corrupted version may already be in git history

### TypeScript: Incomplete Database Types
`read_file()` returns content with `    N|` line-number prefixes embedded. When you write this content back with `write_file()`, the prefixes become part of the file. Subsequent reads compound the corruption: `   1|   2|   3|import...`.

**Symptoms:** Build errors: `Import is not allowed here` with error lines showing `    1|     1|import...`

**Prevention:**
- When editing content, use `patch()` (doesn't re-read full content) or `sed -i` (pure string replacement, no corruption risk)
- If you must use read/write, verify the file is clean afterward: `head -c 100 file | xxd | head -2` — should show no `N|` prefix bytes
- Fix corrupted files: `sed -i 's/^[[:space:]]*[0-9]\+| //' file.tsx`
- After fixing, commit immediately — the corrupted version may already be in git history

### TypeScript: Incomplete Database Types

When adding new Supabase tables (customers, events, tickets, ci_documents, blacklist) that aren't in `src/types/database.ts`, every query against them will get TS errors (type `never`). The existing type file only covers `products`, `categories`, `orders`.

**Quick fix:** `// @ts-nocheck` on line 1 of every affected file (before `'use client'`, not after).

**Better fix:** Expand `src/types/database.ts` to cover all actual tables.

### Next.js 16 Quirks

- **Middleware deprecation:** `middleware.ts` works but shows a deprecation warning (use `proxy` instead in future)
- **Node_modules TS errors:** Non-blocking errors from Next's own type declarations — ignore them, build still succeeds
- **Anonymous cart:** Zustand + `persist` middleware works cross-page but hydration mismatch needs `mounted` guard

### Promo carousels: badge + title + description
- Blog category filtering: accented chars need NFD normalization before comparison
- Dynamic page titles: reset on unmount to prevent stale titles
- Social proof popups: position above WhatsApp float (bottom-32)
- Age gates: use timestamp-based expiry, not boolean localStorage flags
- Stats: initialize with "---" not "0" to avoid flash before animation
- Accessibility: add skip-to-content link with #main-content target
- Filter pagination: reset page to 1 when any filter changes
- Product images: SVG files in /public/images/ exist but es.json must reference them by correct path. "✦" placeholder means the image path is wrong or missing.
- Footer social dedup: if social bar (icon links) AND footer column (text links) both point to same Insta/WA, remove the text link column.
- Title template in layout.tsx shows default even with dynamic page titles -- client components set document.title in useEffect, but SSR output still shows the default until hydration.
- Price range sliders: two stacked range inputs overlap visually when values are close. Use a single range input (max price only) for simplicity.
- Anon review forms: must have `formRef.current?.reset()` + setRating state on submit. Interactive stars need `onClick` handler and conditional `text-warning`/`text-border` classes.
- Accented category names: URLs with accents don't match NFD-normalized comparisons. Always strip combining diacritics when generating URL slugs.
- Guest mode banner: use localStorage with 24h timestamp expiry, not sessionStorage (which shows on every new tab) or simple boolean (which never shows again).
- Page metadata/layout.tsx: the `title` export in metadata is SSR-only. Client-side dynamic titles need a separate `useEffect` with cleanup.
- SVG images on product cards: the SafeImage component renders `<img>` tags. Give the container `class="overflow-hidden"` so the SVG doesn't overflow the gradient background.
- Filter sidebar scroll position: when toggling filters on/off, the sidebar scrolls to top. Acceptable limitation unless building a custom scroll-position cache.
- **Nav links pointing to non-existent pages**: When `es.json` navigation has entries like `/contacto`, `/tienda`, or `/promociones` that don't exist as page files, those nav items silently 404. After any es.json nav change, verify every href by scraping: `for path in ...; do curl -so /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" "https://site.com$path"; done`. Create missing pages as simple content pages — `/tienda` (all products grid), `/promociones` (featured products), `/contacto` (WA + social cards). Client prefered me to fix all product/card grids site-wide: `grid-cols-2 gap-6 sm:grid-cols-2 lg:grid-cols-3`. Always set the base mobile column count explicitly — don't rely on `sm:` breakpoint for mobile users. Check ALL grids on the site when fixing this (homepage feat. products, features, testimonials, tienda, blog, blog category, envio, promociones, nosotros, skeleton).

### Admin UX Deep Roast

When the user says "critic" or "roast" the admin panel visually:

**Method**: Log in as admin, browse every page, take notes. If login is broken (session not persisting), fix that first — see supabase-migration skill's Login/Session fix section.

**Score Framework**: Rate each page on:
- UX (1-10): Can an admin do what they need in ≤3 clicks?
- Visual (1-10): Consistent dark theme, clear hierarchy, not just gray soup
- Performance (1-10): Loads fast, pagination, no "loading..." flash

**Checklist per page**:

Login: Does it redirect after successful auth? Use `window.location.href` not `router.push`. If middleware blocks → move auth checks to component layer.

Sidebar: Grouped into sections? Active item visible? Max 6 per group.

Tables: Paginated (never load 2500 items)? Sticky headers? Search? Empty state with icon?

Forms: Modal/slide-out panel or inline push-down? Validation? Success feedback?

Dashboard: Quick actions? Stats with context (trend arrows, comparison)? "Ver sitio" should open new tab (`target="_blank"`).

Color: Sidebar = bg-gray-950, Header = bg-gray-900, Content = bg-gray-900, Cards = bg-gray-800. Three levels of z-depth.

**Common P0 bugs found in audits**:
1. Login form uses `router.push()` → doesn't sync session cookie → infinite redirect to /login. Fix: `window.location.href = "/mi-cuenta"`
2. Admin pages call `supabase.from()` directly with anon key → 401. Fix: create API routes with service_role key.
3. `docker build` + `docker stack deploy` doesn't restart containers with old CSS. Fix: `docker service update --force`
4. PWA icons 404. Fix: generate with Pillow, then `docker service update --force`
5. sw.js tries to cache POST requests. Fix: `if (event.request.method !== 'GET') return fetch(event.request)`

## Supabase Migration: Inventory-Driven Scoping

When a site has partial custom auth, localStorage-based admin, and build-time static content — and the user wants a full Supabase migration with social login and live admin editing — use the systematic 8-dimension inventory process in `references/supabase-migration-scoping.md`.

The critical finding from El Viajero analysis: **the admin panel's localStorage dependency is always the bigger problem than the auth system**. Admin page rewrites take ~5h vs auth migration at ~2h. Always prioritize identifying what data lives in localStorage vs DB vs JSON files before writing a migration plan.

For a full single-site visual improvement cycle (audit → batch fix → deploy), see `references/single-site-visual-improvement-cycle.md`. This covers: how to critique a live site, prioritize issues by impact, fix content+visuals+types in a single pass, and handle the es.json rewrite + TypeScript cascading failure pitfalls.

After any build or upgrade, run this systematic page-by-page audit. The full 18-point checklist from the Fun4Me session:

1. **Promo carousel**: badge + title + description rendered as one word? Badge needs `mr-1.5`, description needs `ml-2`, title needs proper whitespace.
2. **Blog content**: is the full article content rendering, or just the excerpt? If content lives in es.json, check it's long enough. If stored in a code map, verify all slugs have entries.
3. **Blog WhatsApp URL**: avoid literal template strings (`${encodeURIComponent(post.title)}` in href). Must use proper JS interpolation.
4. **Stats animation**: initial state is "0" or "—"? "0" causes flash of broken numbers. Use "—".
5. **Age gate**: boolean flag or timestamp? Timestamp with 7-day expiry is more resilient.
6. **Social proof popup**: does it overlap the WhatsApp float? Position at `bottom-32` or lower.
7. **Footer**: social platform links appear in both a column AND an icon bar? Remove the text-link column.
8. **Blog category URLs**: accented chars (Guías → guias)? Must NFD-normalize before filter comparison.
9. **Contact page**: has hours, address, multiple topic-specific buttons? Not just a single WhatsApp link.
10. **Privacy/Terms pages**: 8+ substantive sections, not 3-4 one-liners.
11. **Product images**: "✦" anywhere on product cards = image path broken or missing. SVGs should render.
12. **Page titles**: browser tab shows product name, not just site default. Check with `document.title` after hydration.
13. **Breadcrumbs**: visible on product/detail pages, not just hidden JSON-LD in `<head>`.
14. **Accessibility**: skip-to-content link present? `#main-content` target exists? Dark mode toggle has correct `aria-label`?
15. **Duplicate badges**: "Principiante" showing in both LevelBadge and SafetyBadges? Only one should show it.
16. **Price slider**: dual range inputs overlap visually? Replace with single max-price slider.
17. **Anon review form**: reset on submit? Stars are clickable? Form resets with `formRef.current?.reset()`.
18. **Guest mode banner**: shows every new tab (sessionStorage)? Use localStorage with 24h timestamp instead.

## Layout Pattern

Every admin page wraps content in `<AdminShell>` (from `components/admin/admin-layout.tsx`), which:
1. Calls `useAdminAuth()` — checks Supabase session + profiles.role === 'admin'
2. Renders `<AdminSidebar />` on the left (fixed-width, bg-gray-950)

## Auth Flow

```
User clicks /admin/* → middleware.ts checks:
  1. `supabase.auth.getSession()` — if no session → redirect to /login?redirect=/admin/...
  2. `profiles.role === 'admin'` from profile DB — if not admin → redirect to /mi-cuenta
  3. `updateSession()` refreshes cookies ← THIS IS WHERE IT CRASHES IF getAdminClient() isn't routing
```

Login is standard Supabase `signInWithPassword({ email, password })` from `/login` page (rendered server-side by Next.js App Router — no custom auth page in the repo, it uses standard Supabase Auth UI).
